enrichment.short_description: Deel is a global payroll, HR, and Employer of Record platform for hiring and paying employees and contractors in 150+ countries.
enrichment.starting_price: Deel HR is free; Contractor management starts around $49/contractor/month; EOR starts around $599/employee/month; Global Payroll is custom-quoted

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Deel is a global workforce management platform founded in 2019 that lets companies hire, pay, and manage employees and independent contractors in countries around the world, often without needing to set up a local legal entity. It combines Employer of Record (EOR) services, contractor payments, global payroll, and core HR tools into a single system, which is why it's frequently described as an all-in-one platform for international hiring rather than a single-purpose payroll tool.

Under the hood, Deel operates its own network of local entities and compliance partners in many jurisdictions, which it uses to legally employ workers on a client's behalf, generate localized employment contracts, and handle statutory withholdings, benefits, and terminations according to local labor law. Clients interact with this infrastructure through a centralized web dashboard that consolidates worker records, payroll runs, invoices, and compliance documents across every country they operate in.

Deel competes in a crowded EOR and global-payroll category alongside providers like Remote, Papaya Global, and Rippling's global modules. Its main differentiator is breadth: rather than specializing narrowly in payroll or contractor payments, it has expanded into HRIS, equity management, immigration support, and performance/engagement tools, positioning itself as a single system of record for distributed and international teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Deel Is For
paragraphs: Deel is built for companies that want to hire talent in countries where they have no legal entity. Startups and scaleups expanding internationally use its EOR service to onboard employees compliantly within days or weeks instead of the months it typically takes to incorporate a foreign subsidiary.

It also suits mid-market and larger companies that manage sizable networks of international contractors and want contract generation, invoicing, and multi-currency payouts handled through one workflow instead of scattered tools like bank wires, PayPal, or spreadsheets for compliance tracking.

Remote-first and distributed organizations that already operate across multiple countries are a strong fit too, since they can use Deel HR as a free central employee record system and layer on paid EOR, payroll, or contractor modules only where needed, avoiding a patchwork of country-specific vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid Deel
paragraphs: Very small teams hiring only within their home country, with no international employees or contractors, generally don't need Deel's core value proposition and may find simpler, domestic-only payroll or HR software cheaper and easier to run.

Companies with a large number of low-cost contractors and a tight budget should model costs carefully, since per-contractor and per-employee monthly fees can add up quickly at scale compared to handling payments directly or through a lower-cost invoicing tool.

Organizations that need deep customization of payroll rules, tight integration with an existing on-premise HRIS, or highly specialized in-country payroll providers for regulatory reasons may find Deel's standardized, platform-driven approach less flexible than working with local specialists directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Deel's Employer of Record product lets a business legally engage full-time employees abroad without opening a local entity, with Deel acting as the legal employer of record for tax, benefits, and termination purposes while the client directs the person's day-to-day work.

Its contractor management tools generate localized agreements, automate invoicing and approvals, and support payouts through multiple methods including bank transfer, digital wallets, and cards, while its global payroll product runs payroll for a company's own entities across several countries from a single dashboard.

Beyond payments, Deel offers a free HRIS for centralized employee records and org charts, plus add-on modules for equity management, immigration and visa sponsorship, background checks, and performance/engagement tools (Deel Engage), covering much of the employee lifecycle beyond just pay.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Deel Experience
paragraphs: Getting started typically involves creating a worker profile, choosing the engagement type (EOR employee, contractor, or payroll-only), and letting Deel generate a compliant contract based on the worker's country. Onboarding is largely guided through forms rather than requiring legal or HR expertise from the client.

Day-to-day, admins use the dashboard to approve invoices or timesheets, trigger payroll runs, and track costs across countries, while workers use a companion portal and mobile app to submit time off, view pay stubs, and manage payout methods. Support is primarily handled through in-app chat and a help center, with dedicated account support typically reserved for larger or EOR/payroll customers.

Overall, the experience trades some flexibility for consolidation: teams get one login and one invoice for work that would otherwise span several country-specific vendors, but the tradeoff is a platform-standardized process and a cost structure that scales with headcount, which is worth weighing against a company's specific mix of employees and contractors.

enrichment.short_description: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab offering a free chat app and low-cost API built on its open-weight DeepSeek-V3 and R1 reasoning models.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DeepSeek is an AI research lab and product suite based in Hangzhou, China, founded in 2023 as a spinoff of High-Flyer, a Chinese quantitative hedge fund. The company builds large language models and makes them available through a free web and mobile chat application as well as a pay-per-use developer API, positioning itself as a lower-cost alternative to the more established US AI labs.

Its two best-known model families are DeepSeek-V3, a general-purpose large language model built on a mixture-of-experts architecture, and DeepSeek-R1, a reasoning-oriented model that displays its intermediate chain-of-thought before producing a final answer. Both are released as open-weight models, meaning the underlying weights can be downloaded and run on a company's own hardware rather than only through DeepSeek's hosted service.

DeepSeek drew global attention in January 2025 when the release of R1 coincided with the company's own claims of having trained a competitive reasoning model at a much lower cost than commonly reported for comparable US models. Those claims, which DeepSeek has not independently substantiated with detailed audited figures, contributed to a sharp reaction in AI-related stock markets and renewed debate about the assumptions behind frontier model training costs. Regardless of how those specific figures hold up, the release established DeepSeek as a serious entrant in the global large language model landscape.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who DeepSeek Is For
paragraphs: Everyday users who want a capable, no-cost chatbot for writing, research, summarizing, and general question-answering are a natural fit, since the core chat product does not require payment to access either the standard model or the reasoning mode.

Developers and technical teams building AI-powered features on a budget are another strong match, given the API's usage-based pricing has generally undercut comparable offerings from larger US providers, making it attractive for prototyping or cost-sensitive production workloads.

Students, researchers, and engineers working on math, coding, or logic-heavy problems can benefit from R1's visible step-by-step reasoning, which makes it easier to follow how the model arrived at an answer and to catch errors in its reasoning chain. Organizations and researchers who want to self-host or fine-tune an open model also gain from DeepSeek's willingness to publish model weights rather than keeping them fully closed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Organizations with strict data residency, privacy, or regulatory requirements may want to avoid routing sensitive data through DeepSeek's hosted chat or API, since the company and its infrastructure are based in China and subject to Chinese law, which is a common concern raised by security teams and some government agencies.

Enterprises that need guaranteed uptime service-level agreements, dedicated account support, formal compliance certifications, or established vendor-management relationships may find DeepSeek's support infrastructure comparatively thin next to larger, more established AI vendors.

Users who need to discuss politically sensitive topics, particularly those related to Chinese government policy or history, are likely to encounter content restrictions and refusals, since the hosted chat app applies moderation aligned with Chinese regulatory requirements. Teams that prioritize a mature third-party plugin and integration ecosystem may also find DeepSeek's surrounding tooling less developed than that of longer-established competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: The core product is a chat interface, available on the web and as iOS and Android apps, that lets users switch between a standard conversational mode and a reasoning mode (often labeled as a 'DeepThink' or R1 mode) that shows the model's intermediate reasoning steps before giving a final answer. A web search toggle lets the assistant pull in current information rather than relying solely on its training data.

Beyond the chat app, DeepSeek publishes open model weights for versions of its V3 and R1 model families, allowing developers and researchers to download, self-host, and fine-tune the models under their own infrastructure and licensing terms rather than depending on DeepSeek's servers.

For developers, DeepSeek offers an API compatible with common request formats used by other major LLM providers, billed on a usage basis by token volume, along with support for file and document upload in chat, multi-turn conversation history, and strong performance on coding and mathematics tasks relative to its cost, based on independent benchmark comparisons published after its releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: The DeepSeek Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, the chat interface is intentionally simple: a single input box, a toggle for reasoning mode, and a toggle for web search, without the layered menus or add-on marketplaces found in some competing chat apps. Response times are generally fast for standard queries, while reasoning-mode answers take longer as the model works through visible intermediate steps.

Because usage surged rapidly after DeepSeek's model releases went viral, the service has at times experienced slower responses or temporary outages during periods of heavy demand, which is worth factoring in for time-sensitive or production use cases that depend on the hosted chat or API rather than a self-hosted deployment.

Overall, DeepSeek positions itself as a value-oriented option in the AI assistant space: a free, capable chatbot for individuals, an inexpensive API for developers, and openly published model weights for teams that want more control, trading some of the polish, support depth, and ecosystem maturity of larger incumbents for lower cost and more openness.

enrichment.short_description: Browser-based webinar platform for marketers offering live, automated, series, and on-demand events with no download required.
enrichment.starting_price: From $45/month per host billed annually (Starter plan, up to 50 attendees); $99/month if billed monthly
enrichment.company_size: Small team (11-50 employees), operated as a product of Banzai International, Inc.
enrichment.headquarters: Cody, Wyoming, USA (parent company Banzai International, Inc.)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Demio is a browser-based webinar and virtual event platform built for marketing teams that use webinars as a demand-generation and customer-education channel. Instead of requiring a desktop app or plugin, both hosts and attendees join a Demio room directly from a web browser, which removes a common source of drop-off in webinar funnels. The platform is positioned less as a general video-conferencing tool and more as a purpose-built environment for scheduled, promoted webinar events.

The product supports several event formats from a single dashboard: live webinars run in real time, series events repeat a session across multiple scheduled sessions, automated (sometimes called evergreen) webinars replay a pre-recorded session on a set schedule while still surfacing timed polls and calls to action, and on-demand webinars let visitors watch a recording whenever they choose. This range lets a marketing team reuse one core presentation across several delivery styles without rebuilding it each time.

Demio also includes registration page creation, attendee engagement features such as polls, handouts, and calls to action, post-event analytics, and integrations with common marketing automation and CRM tools. It was acquired by Banzai International in 2021 and continues to operate as a distinct product under that company, which also owns other event and engagement marketing tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Demio Is For
paragraphs: Demio is best suited to B2B marketing and demand-generation teams that run webinars as a recurring part of their funnel, such as product demos, lead-generation events, or customer onboarding sessions. Its combination of registration pages, engagement prompts, and analytics maps closely to how marketers already think about webinars as a conversion channel rather than just a meeting format.

SaaS companies and agencies that want to turn a single presentation into an ongoing, semi-automated lead source are a strong fit, since the automated and series webinar formats let a recorded session run repeatedly with minimal manual effort while still looking live to attendees. Course creators and coaches running smaller cohorts also use Demio for its simplicity and no-download attendee experience.

Teams that already have a webinar workflow built around simple, single-session live events but want cleaner registration pages, better engagement tools, and more polished analytics than a generic video-call tool provides will find Demio a natural upgrade without a steep learning curve for either hosts or attendees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Organizations planning large-scale virtual conferences with multiple concurrent tracks, expo-style networking, or thousands of simultaneous attendees on a single event are likely to outgrow what Demio is designed for; it is built around single-room webinar sessions rather than multi-track conference production.

Teams that need deep learning-management functionality, such as structured course sequences, quizzes, certificates, or drip-fed curricula, should look at a dedicated online course or LMS platform instead, since Demio's automated and series formats are aimed at marketing webinars rather than formal education delivery.

Budget-sensitive solo users or very early-stage founders who want a permanently free plan should also be cautious, since Demio does not offer an indefinite free tier; access is limited to a time-boxed free trial before a paid plan is required, and attendee capacity is tiered by plan, which can push costs up quickly as an audience grows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the core of Demio is its no-download webinar room: attendees click a join link and enter the session directly in their browser on desktop or mobile, while hosts get a control panel for managing slides, video, chat, and audience interaction without separate software. This is paired with registration pages that can be customized and embedded to capture sign-ups ahead of an event.

Engagement features are a major focus, including live polls, downloadable handouts, and timed calls to action that can appear during a session to drive clicks toward a pricing page, booking link, or offer. In automated and series events, these same engagement elements can be scheduled to appear at fixed points in the recording, so a pre-recorded session still behaves interactively for the viewer.

Beyond the room itself, Demio provides post-event analytics covering registration and attendance rates, engagement actions taken during the session, and drop-off points, along with integrations to marketing automation platforms and CRMs so registrant and attendance data can flow into a team's existing stack. A documented API and Zapier integration extend this further for teams that want custom workflows around registrations and follow-up.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Demio Experience
paragraphs: Setting up an event in Demio typically starts with choosing an event type, building a registration page from a template, and configuring the session itself, including uploaded slides or video and any polls, handouts, or calls to action to trigger during the presentation. The interface is generally described as clean and modern compared to older webinar tools, with most core actions reachable from a single dashboard.

During a live session, the host view separates presentation controls from audience management, so a presenter can advance slides or trigger a call to action while a co-host or moderator handles incoming chat questions. Attendees see a simplified, distraction-light room focused on the presentation, chat, and any active engagement elements, with no login or app install required to participate.

After an event ends, hosts land on a reporting view summarizing attendance, engagement, and registrant activity, which is where the platform's marketing orientation is most visible: the emphasis throughout the experience is on turning webinar attendance into measurable pipeline activity rather than simply hosting a video call.

enrichment.short_description: Deputy is workforce management software for shift-based teams, covering employee scheduling, time tracking, tasking, and team messaging.
enrichment.starting_price: From $5 USD per user/month (Lite plan, billed monthly)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Deputy is a workforce management platform built for businesses that schedule hourly, shift-based employees. Founded in 2008 and headquartered in Sydney, Australia, the company grew out of the everyday problem of coordinating rosters, timesheets, and staff communication for retail and hospitality teams, and has since expanded into healthcare, hospitality, and other shift-heavy industries worldwide.

At its core, Deputy combines four functions in one system: employee scheduling, time and attendance tracking, task management, and team communication. Managers build schedules from a web dashboard or the mobile app, staff clock in and out through the mobile app, a tablet kiosk, or a browser, and shift notes and announcements flow through the same tool employees already check for their upcoming shifts.

Deputy is not a full payroll processor or a broad HR information system on its own; it is scoped around the scheduling-and-timekeeping layer, then extends outward through integrations with payroll providers such as Gusto, QuickBooks, Xero, and ADP, plus its own Deputy Payroll option in the US delivered via a partnership with Paycor. That makes it best understood as a scheduling and timekeeping hub that plugs into whatever payroll stack a business already runs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Deputy Is For
paragraphs: Deputy is aimed at operations managers, store or restaurant owners, and HR teams responsible for building repeating shift rosters for hourly staff. Retail chains, cafes, restaurants, hotels, and healthcare or aged-care providers with fluctuating, demand-driven staffing needs are the clearest fit, especially once a business runs more than one location and needs a consistent way to build and publish schedules.

It also suits businesses that care about labor-law compliance, since Deputy can flag rest-break violations, overtime thresholds, and award or union pay-rule conflicts before a schedule is published. Multi-location employers who need visibility across sites, and who want staff to self-manage availability, shift swaps, and leave requests from their phones, tend to get the most value from the platform.

Growing companies that expect to add more automation over time are a good match too, because Deputy's tiered plans let a business start with basic scheduling and timesheets, then step up to auto-scheduling, demand forecasting, and labor-cost budgeting as headcount and complexity increase, without switching systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Companies with a fully salaried, non-shift workforce -- most software, agency, or professional-services teams, for example -- generally don't need Deputy's rostering and time-clock machinery and are better served by a lighter HR or project-tracking tool. If nobody in the business clocks in against a schedule, Deputy's core value proposition mostly disappears.

Very small, single-location teams that are highly cost-sensitive may also find it a poor fit: Deputy has no permanent free plan, and a minimum monthly account spend applies regardless of headcount, which can make the effective per-user cost high for a handful of part-time staff. Businesses whose admin staff need a fully localized back-office experience should also note that the web dashboard is English-only; only the mobile apps offer other languages.

Finally, organizations that want one vendor to run scheduling, time tracking, and full payroll natively end-to-end may be disappointed that Deputy's own payroll product is currently US-specific and delivered through a partner rather than built entirely in-house, meaning payroll in other regions still runs through a separate connected provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Scheduling is the anchor feature: managers build shifts with drag-and-drop tools, copy previous weeks, apply templates, and account for staff availability and qualifications, while employees see their upcoming shifts and can request swaps or additional coverage from the app. Time and attendance builds on the same schedule data, letting staff clock in and out via mobile, a shared tablet kiosk with photo capture, or a browser, with timesheets that reconcile automatically against the planned roster.

On top of scheduling and time tracking, Deputy layers task management (checklists tied to a shift, such as opening or closing duties), a news-feed style communication tool for shift notes and company announcements, and leave management for time-off requests and approvals. Higher plan tiers add auto-scheduling suggestions and demand forecasting that use historical sales or foot-traffic data to recommend staffing levels, along with wage and labor budget tracking to keep shift costs inside a target percentage.

Compliance and reporting round out the feature set: built-in alerts for rest breaks, overtime, and award or union rules help reduce costly scheduling mistakes, while reporting dashboards and an optional analytics add-on surface labor-cost and attendance trends. Deputy also connects to a broad set of payroll, point-of-sale, and HR systems, so scheduling and time data can flow into whatever back-office software a business already relies on.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Deputy Experience
paragraphs: For managers, the day-to-day experience centers on a schedule-building screen that feels closer to a calendar app than a spreadsheet: shifts can be dragged into place, copied from a prior week, and published to the whole team in a couple of clicks. Onboarding a new location generally means importing staff, setting availability and pay rules, and building the first roster, which most teams can do without vendor help.

For frontline employees, the mobile app is the main touchpoint -- checking upcoming shifts, requesting a swap or time off, clocking in when a shift starts, and reading shift notes or team announcements all happen in the same place, which cuts down on separate group chats or printed schedules pinned to a break-room wall.

Overall, Deputy reads as a purpose-built tool rather than a general-purpose suite: it does scheduling, time tracking, tasking, and shift communication well, and it is honest about routing full payroll processing through partners rather than owning that layer everywhere. Businesses evaluating it should weigh the tiered pricing and add-on costs against how much of the automation (auto-scheduling, demand forecasting, analytics) they actually need on day one versus later.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Descript is an audio and video editing application built around a deceptively simple idea: if a computer can transcribe what's being said, you should be able to edit the recording by editing the text. Instead of scrubbing a timeline frame by frame, users cut, rearrange, or delete words in a transcript, and the underlying audio or video updates to match. That core mechanic turns editing into something closer to word processing, which is a meaningful shift for anyone who records a lot of spoken-word content but doesn't have formal training in traditional non-linear editing software.

The company was founded in 2017 by Andrew Mason (previously known as the co-founder and CEO of Groupon) and is based in San Francisco. Descript started as a transcription and podcast-editing tool and has since expanded into a broader production suite that includes screen recording, multitrack video editing, AI voice generation through a feature called Overdub, and automated audio cleanup via Studio Sound. More recently the product has layered in a set of AI-assisted editing tools, sometimes referred to under the internal branding "Underlord," aimed at automating repetitive editing tasks like removing filler words or silences.

Descript positions itself less as a replacement for high-end professional editing suites and more as an accessible, faster path to a finished podcast episode, talking-head video, tutorial, or marketing clip. It runs as a desktop app (Mac and Windows) with cloud-synced projects, so editing, transcription, and AI processing happen with an internet connection even though the interface itself is a native application.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Descript Is For
paragraphs: Descript is best suited to people who produce a steady volume of spoken-word content and want to spend less time on the mechanical parts of editing. Podcasters are the clearest fit: transcript-based editing makes it fast to cut dead air, remove tangents, and tighten interviews, while Studio Sound and Overdub help clean up inconsistent recording conditions across remote guests.

YouTubers, course creators, and educators who film themselves talking to a camera also benefit, particularly from features like automatic filler-word removal and the Eye Contact tool, which nudges on-camera gaze toward the lens even when the speaker was reading a script off to the side. For these creators, Descript can meaningfully shorten the gap between a raw recording and a publishable video.

Marketing and communications teams that produce webinars, product demos, internal training videos, or short social clips are another strong match, since Descript's screen recording, templated exports for social formats, and shared cloud workspace make it easier for non-editors on a team to contribute to video production without learning a full NLE.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Professional film and broadcast editors who need frame-accurate trimming, advanced color grading, layered VFX compositing, or deep audio mixing with plugin chains will likely find Descript's toolset comparatively shallow next to dedicated tools like Premiere Pro, DaVinci Resolve, or Pro Tools. Descript can get a project most of the way there, but it isn't built to be the final stop for high-end post-production.

Because Descript's AI features and project sync depend on cloud processing, users working with very long recordings, large 4K video files, or unreliable internet connections may run into slower transcription and rendering times than they would in a fully local, offline editing workflow.

Anyone expecting unlimited functionality on the free tier, or teams with tight budgets needing many seats with full AI features, should look closely at plan limits before committing, since features like Overdub, Studio Sound, and extended transcription time are generally gated behind paid plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: The centerpiece of Descript is transcript-based editing: recordings are automatically transcribed, and deleting, moving, or retyping words in that transcript edits the underlying audio or video in place. This is paired with a more conventional multitrack timeline for users who want finer manual control over layering clips, music, and sound effects alongside the text view.

Overdub is Descript's AI voice-cloning feature, letting a user train a synthetic version of their own voice (with consent) to generate new narration or patch over a mispronounced word without a re-recording session. Studio Sound applies AI-based noise reduction and audio enhancement to make recordings sound closer to studio quality, which is particularly useful for remote interviews recorded on inconsistent microphones. Filler word removal and silence removal automate two of the most tedious parts of rough-cut editing, and the Eye Contact feature adjusts a speaker's gaze in video to appear more directly at the camera.

Beyond the core editor, Descript includes built-in screen recording for tutorials and demos, publishing and export options aimed at podcast platforms and social video formats, ready-made templates for short-form social clips, and collaboration tools such as shared workspaces, comments, and version history so teams can review and edit projects together in the cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Descript Experience
paragraphs: Using Descript feels closer to working in a word processor than in a traditional video editor, which is by design. New users typically import or record a file, wait for automatic transcription, and then start editing directly in the text panel, with the timeline view available for anything that requires more visual precision. The learning curve is noticeably shallower than tools like Premiere Pro or Final Cut Pro, especially for anyone already comfortable editing documents.

Because projects live in the cloud, switching between the desktop app and collaborating with teammates is generally smooth, and changes sync automatically. That said, very large video files or long-form recordings can slow down transcription and AI processing, and users have reported that performance depends noticeably on file size, internet connection, and how much AI-driven processing (like Studio Sound or Overdub) is applied.

Descript maintains documentation, tutorials, and an active user community to help people get comfortable with its more advanced AI features, and the product continues to add new AI-assisted editing capabilities over time, which means the interface and feature set can shift meaningfully between visits for less frequent users.

enrichment.short_description: Devin by Cognition is an autonomous AI coding agent that plans, writes, tests, and ships code end-to-end inside its own sandboxed dev environment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Devin is an autonomous AI software engineer built by Cognition, a company founded in 2023. Rather than acting as a code-completion sidekick that suggests the next line as a developer types, Devin is designed to take a task described in plain language and carry it through to a finished, testable result on its own. It works inside a sandboxed environment that includes its own shell, code editor, and browser, which lets it read a codebase, plan a sequence of steps, and then actually execute those steps instead of only describing them.

In practice, Devin clones the relevant repository, forms a plan for the assigned task, writes code, runs the project's existing test suite, and iterates when something fails, much the way a human engineer would debug their own change before asking for review. When the work is ready, Devin opens a pull request with a summary of what it did and why, so a human reviewer can approve, request changes, or take over. Every action it takes inside its sandbox is logged and replayable, which gives engineers visibility into how a result was reached rather than just the end diff.

Devin is built to plug into workflows teams already use: it connects to GitHub for code and pull requests, and to tools like Slack, Linear, and Jira so tasks can be assigned from a ticket or a chat message. Cognition also ships companion tools alongside the core agent, including Devin Review for automated pull request feedback and DeepWiki for auto-generated codebase documentation. In 2025, Cognition also acquired Windsurf, the AI-native code editor, though Windsurf remains a separate interactive coding product distinct from Devin's autonomous, task-based agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Devin Is For
paragraphs: Devin fits engineering teams that have a backlog of well-scoped, repetitive work: dependency and framework upgrades, large-scale refactors, flaky test fixes, and migrating code between languages or platforms. These tasks tend to have a clear definition of done, which is exactly the kind of assignment an autonomous agent can verify against a test suite and hand back as a reviewable pull request rather than an open-ended conversation.

It's also a strong fit for organizations already standardized on GitHub, Slack, Linear, or Jira, since Devin can be assigned work directly from an existing ticket or channel without changing how the team plans its work. Teams maintaining large or older codebases that need steady modernization, rather than a single rewrite, get particular value from an agent that can work through many similar tickets in parallel across a monorepo or multiple repositories.

Individual senior engineers and tech leads can also use Devin as an extra set of hands for the engineering toil that would otherwise interrupt deeper work: cleaning up boilerplate, writing one-off migration scripts, or triaging a stack of minor bug reports. In that setup, Devin absorbs the lower-judgment tasks while the human stays focused on architecture and code review.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Devin relies heavily on being able to verify its own work, so codebases with little or no automated testing and CI give it much less to check its output against, and results in that setting are harder to trust without close review. Teams working in early-stage, rapidly changing prototypes with no established conventions may find that the agent has less structure to plan against than it does in a mature, well-tested repository.

Developers who mainly want fast, line-by-line pairing while they type, rather than delegating a whole task, are likely better served by an inline coding assistant or IDE-based tool. Devin's model is built around independent, multi-step sessions rather than continuous keystroke-level suggestions, so it solves a different problem than a typical autocomplete-style assistant.

Budget-conscious individuals and hobbyists making many small, exploratory changes may also find that Devin's metered, credit-based usage adds up faster than a flat-rate subscription tool would for the same volume of small edits. Because billing is tied to active agent work rather than a fixed per-seat rate alone, costs are less predictable for workloads that consist of many short, ad hoc sessions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the core of Devin is its planning and execution loop: given a task description, it breaks the work into steps, then carries them out inside its own sandboxed shell, editor, and browser rather than only producing suggested code. This lets it install dependencies, run scripts, browse documentation, and re-run tests as part of completing a task, closing the loop between writing code and confirming it works.

Around that core agent, Devin integrates with the tools engineering teams already run day to day: GitHub for repositories and pull requests, Slack and Linear for assigning and tracking tasks, and the Model Context Protocol for connecting additional internal or third-party tools. Companion products extend this further, with Devin Review offering automated pull request feedback and DeepWiki generating and maintaining codebase documentation that engineers can search.

For larger organizations, Devin's enterprise tier adds administrative and security controls on top of the core agent, including SAML and OIDC single sign-on, centralized admin management, and the option of a dedicated deployment. Session recordings and step-by-step logs are available across plans, giving reviewers a full account of the commands, edits, and test runs behind any given pull request.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Devin Experience
paragraphs: Getting started with Devin means connecting a GitHub repository through Cognition's app, after which Devin indexes the codebase so it has context on the project's structure and conventions. From there, a task is assigned much like it would be to a teammate: a short description in a chat window, a Slack message, or an existing ticket, rather than a detailed technical specification.

Once a session starts, engineers can watch Devin work in something close to real time, seeing the shell commands it runs, the files it edits, and the pages it visits in its sandboxed browser. Users can step in to redirect the approach, answer a clarifying question, or simply let the session run unattended and check back once it opens a pull request with a summary of the change.

How well that experience lands tends to depend on the codebase it's pointed at: engineers report the strongest results on repetitive, clearly scoped tickets in projects with decent test coverage, where Devin's own test runs give confidence in the result. Tasks that hinge on ambiguous product decisions or unwritten architectural context still need a human to weigh in, which is consistent with Cognition positioning Devin as a collaborator that takes on bounded engineering work rather than a fully independent replacement for a developer.

faqs.question: Does Devin replace human developers?

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dext is a cloud-based bookkeeping automation platform that removes manual data entry from the process of recording receipts, invoices, and bank transactions. It was founded in 2010 in London as Receipt Bank, and after a decade of building tools for accountants and bookkeepers, the company rebranded to Dext in 2020 to reflect a broader product line that goes beyond simple receipt scanning. Dext was acquired by private equity firm Hg in 2021 and, more recently, by IRIS Software Group in late 2024, placing it inside a larger portfolio of accounting and compliance software.

The core workflow is straightforward: a user submits a document by snapping a photo in the mobile app, forwarding an email, uploading a PDF, or connecting a bank feed. Dext's OCR and AI engine then reads the document, pulls out the supplier, date, line items, and tax detail, and lets the user (or their bookkeeper) review and approve the extracted data before it is pushed into an accounting platform such as Xero, QuickBooks, or Sage. The goal is to turn a shoebox of paper receipts, or an inbox full of PDF invoices, into structured, categorized transactions with minimal typing.

Dext is really a family of products rather than a single tool. Dext Prepare handles capture and extraction, Dext Precision (built on the Xavier Analytics acquisition) runs data-quality and compliance checks across a bookkeeper's client base, and Dext Commerce pulls in sales data from e-commerce and payment platforms. The product is sold both directly to businesses and, heavily, through accounting and bookkeeping firms who resell or bundle it as part of their client services, which shapes a lot of its feature set and pricing structure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dext Is For
paragraphs: Dext is built first and foremost for accounting and bookkeeping practices that process paperwork for many clients at once. Firms that want a single dashboard to monitor document flow, chase clients for missing receipts, and keep a consistent data pipeline into Xero, QuickBooks, or Sage across their whole client roster are the platform's core audience, and most of its practice-tier features (multi-client views, workflow controls, Precision's anomaly checks) are designed with that use case in mind.

Small and mid-sized businesses that already use one of Dext's supported accounting platforms are a strong secondary fit, particularly ones with a steady volume of receipts, supplier invoices, or expense claims from a team of employees. Retailers, hospitality operators, trades businesses, and professional services firms that want to stop chasing paper receipts and manually keying invoice line items can use Dext directly, without going through a bookkeeper.

Finance teams and bookkeepers who care about the accuracy of the numbers going into the ledger, not just the speed of getting them there, are also well served, since Dext Precision is specifically aimed at flagging duplicate entries, missing information, and other data-quality issues before they cause problems at tax time or during an audit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very low-volume users, such as sole traders or freelancers who handle only a handful of receipts a month, often find Dext's recurring subscription cost hard to justify next to simpler or cheaper receipt-capture tools, since Dext is priced and packaged around a minimum tier of users and document volume rather than true pay-as-you-go usage.

Businesses that don't use one of Dext's supported accounting platforms will lose much of the platform's value, because the payoff of automated capture depends on a clean sync into the general ledger; without that integration, Dext becomes a more expensive document scanner. Similarly, companies looking for an all-in-one accounting or invoicing suite should look elsewhere, as Dext is a data-capture and document-management layer that sits alongside accounting software, not a replacement for it.

Organizations that primarily deal in non-English or heavily handwritten documents, or that need extensive custom workflow logic beyond what Dext's rules and approval settings support, may also run into more manual correction and less flexibility than they expect, since the product's extraction accuracy and configurability are strongest around standard typed invoices and receipts in its core supported markets.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Dext captures documents through several channels: a mobile app for photographing receipts on the go, a dedicated forwarding email address for digital invoices, a browser-based upload, and direct bank feed connections for statement data. Once a document comes in, Dext's OCR and AI extraction reads the supplier name, date, amounts, tax, and line items, and applies supplier- or category-based rules so recurring expenses get coded consistently without the user re-entering the same details each time.

On the integration side, Dext connects to a large roster of accounting platforms, including Xero, QuickBooks, and Sage, and supports connections to thousands of banks for statement fetching, so extracted data flows directly into the correct ledger accounts rather than sitting in a separate silo. Document storage (sometimes referred to as Dext's Vault) keeps a long-term digital archive of every receipt and invoice, which is useful for audit trails and tax record-keeping requirements.

For accounting and bookkeeping firms, Dext Precision adds a compliance layer on top of the raw data, scanning client ledgers for anomalies such as duplicate transactions, missing invoices, or inconsistent coding, and surfacing them in a practice-wide dashboard. Additional capabilities include expense report submission and approval workflows for employee reimbursements, supplier statement reconciliation to match statements against recorded invoices, and Dext Commerce for pulling in e-commerce and payment platform sales data alongside standard expense documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Dext Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Dext typically means snapping a photo of a receipt or forwarding an invoice email and then, periodically, reviewing a queue of extracted documents to confirm the supplier, category, and amounts before they're pushed to the connected accounting platform. For businesses with straightforward, standard-format documents, this review step is usually quick; more unusual formats, handwriting, or non-English text tend to require more manual correction.

Because Dext is so often deployed and configured by an accounting or bookkeeping firm on a client's behalf, the setup experience can feel different depending on who's driving it. A practice managing the connection, workflow rules, and chart-of-accounts mapping tends to have a smoother rollout than a business owner setting everything up alone for the first time, where getting integration mappings and approval rules right can take some trial and error.

Support is delivered through Dext's help centre, in-app guidance, and, for many customers, their accounting or bookkeeping partner rather than direct vendor contact. The mobile apps are generally straightforward for the core job of photographing and submitting a receipt, while the web dashboard is where most of the review, correction, and (for practices) multi-client oversight happens.

faqs.question: What does Dext actually do?
faqs.answer: Dext captures receipts, invoices, and bank statements through its mobile app, email forwarding, or bank connections, uses OCR and AI to extract the key data from each document, and syncs that data into accounting software like Xero, QuickBooks, or Sage.

faqs.question: Is Dext the same company as Receipt Bank?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dext is the rebranded name of Receipt Bank, a bookkeeping automation company founded in London in 2010. It adopted the Dext name in 2020 as it expanded beyond receipt capture into data-quality and analytics tools.

faqs.question: What accounting software does Dext integrate with?
faqs.answer: Dext integrates with a range of accounting platforms, most notably Xero, QuickBooks, and Sage, allowing extracted receipt, invoice, and bank data to sync directly into a business's existing ledger.

faqs.question: Does Dext have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Dext does not offer a permanent free tier. It provides a 14-day free trial without requiring a credit card, after which users choose a paid plan based on user count and document volume.

faqs.question: Who owns Dext now?
faqs.answer: Dext was acquired by private equity firm Hg in 2021 and, following that, became part of IRIS Software Group after an acquisition in late 2024.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud business phone, video meetings, and AI-powered contact center platform with real-time call transcription, sentiment analysis, and coaching.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dialpad is a cloud-based business communications platform founded in 2011 and headquartered in San Francisco. It combines a hosted business phone system with video meetings, team messaging, and an AI contact center in one browser-based and mobile app, replacing traditional on-premise PBX hardware with a subscription service.

What distinguishes Dialpad from most VoIP competitors is Dialpad Ai, a real-time voice intelligence layer built into every call. It transcribes conversations live, flags sentiment shifts, surfaces coaching prompts to reps during a call, and generates post-call summaries and action items automatically, without needing a separate transcription add-on.

The platform is sold as several connected products: Dialpad Ai Voice for business phone service, Dialpad Ai Contact Center for support and sales teams, and Dialpad Ai Meetings for video conferencing, all sharing the same admin console, analytics layer, and AI engine so data and insights carry across channels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Dialpad Is For
paragraphs: Dialpad fits small and mid-sized businesses that want to retire a legacy desk-phone system, or a patchwork of separate calling, meeting, and messaging tools, in favor of one cloud subscription that works from a laptop, browser, or phone app.

Sales and support teams get particular value from the built-in AI: call summaries save reps from manual note-taking, sentiment tracking helps managers spot at-risk conversations, and coaching cues can nudge new hires toward better talk tracks during a live call rather than only in a review afterward.

Distributed and remote-first companies also suit Dialpad well, since every extension, voicemail, and call recording lives in the cloud and is reachable from any device, and admins can provision or reassign numbers between employees without shipping hardware or scheduling a technician visit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small teams or solo operators who only need a couple of numbers may find Dialpad's per-user pricing and plan minimums, three users on the Pro plan and considerably more on Enterprise, less economical than a lighter-weight virtual number app built for one or two users.

Organizations with heavy on-premise telephony investments, complex legacy PBX integrations, or strict data-residency requirements outside Dialpad's supported regions may find migration more disruptive than the AI features are worth, at least until those constraints are resolved.

Teams that mainly need a simple, no-frills calling app without transcription, coaching, or analytics might find Dialpad's interface and admin console more feature-dense than necessary, and could get comparable core calling functionality for less from a stripped-down competitor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the center of the platform is Dialpad Ai, which transcribes calls and meetings in real time, generates automatic summaries and action items, tracks call sentiment, and can surface live coaching cards, such as compliance reminders or objection-handling tips, while a conversation is still happening.

On the phone system side, Dialpad provides standard business calling features, auto-attendants, call routing and ring groups, local and toll-free numbers, number porting, voicemail transcription, and calling within supported countries, delivered through desktop, web, and mobile apps rather than desk hardware.

The Ai Contact Center adds queue management, supervisor coaching tools, and post-call analytics for support teams, while Ai Meetings covers video conferencing with the same live transcription and summaries, and native integrations with Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, and HubSpot help the platform fit into existing workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Dialpad Experience
paragraphs: Setup is largely self-service: admins can port existing numbers, assign extensions, and configure call routing from a web-based console without needing a dedicated telecom technician, and most users are placing and receiving calls from the desktop or mobile app the same day.

Day-to-day, the AI layer is the most noticeable part of the experience: live transcripts scroll alongside a call, a summary and follow-up items appear in the call log within moments of hanging up, and managers can review sentiment trends across a team without listening to every recording.

Reporting and admin tools are comprehensive enough for larger teams to track call volume, quality, and agent performance, though that same depth means new admins typically need some time to learn the console, and the richer AI coaching and contact-center capabilities are concentrated in the higher-priced plans.

faqs.question: Does Dialpad offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dialpad offers free trial periods on its paid plans so teams can test calling, messaging, and AI features before committing to a subscription. Trial length and terms are set by Dialpad and may vary by plan or region.

faqs.question: Can I keep my existing business phone number?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dialpad supports number porting, so existing local and toll-free numbers can typically be transferred from a previous carrier into the platform during setup.

faqs.question: What does Dialpad Ai actually do during a call?
faqs.answer: Dialpad Ai transcribes the conversation in real time, tracks sentiment, can surface coaching prompts to the agent while the call is happening, and produces a summary with action items once the call ends.

faqs.question: Does Dialpad integrate with CRM tools like Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Dialpad offers native integrations with CRMs such as Salesforce and HubSpot, along with Google Workspace and Microsoft 365, though some integrations are gated to Pro-tier plans and above.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Dialpad's Standard, Pro, and Enterprise plans?
faqs.answer: Standard covers core calling and AI transcription with no minimum seat requirement, Pro adds CRM integrations and international messaging with a three-user minimum, and Enterprise adds SSO, unlimited ring groups, and an uptime SLA at a custom quoted price with a much higher user minimum.
